visual_notes: Slow push on the anchor plate. Keep the muslin cloth, notebook, and play gym all visible in frame.
narration: If you've ever played peekaboo and felt like your baby somehow knew what was coming, that intuition is backed by real developmental psychology.

visual_notes: Keynote export built on the four-vignette Nano Banana plate. Hold long enough to read the four labels.
narration: Long before babies can talk, they're already predicting social patterns, detecting cause and effect, and storing simple memories of new actions.

visual_notes: Stay on the same overlay and add a very light Ken Burns push.
narration: In this video, I'll walk through four classic infant experiments you can adapt at home, what each one reveals, and how to do them without turning a playful observation into an overblown conclusion. These are all simplified home versions of famous lab paradigms, which means the magic is not special equipment. It's consistency, observation, and knowing what not to overclaim.

visual_notes: Ground-rules Keynote export over the calm setup plate.
narration: First, the big caveat: these are not diagnostic tests.

visual_notes: Use the uncluttered setup plate without extra text for a beat of breathing room.
narration: A single home session cannot tell you whether your baby is advanced, delayed, or anything in between. Think of these as carefully observed games.

visual_notes: Return to the overlay with short practical rules visible on screen.
on_screen_text: Calm baby • quiet room • stop early • keep it short
narration: For all four, use the same ground rules: choose a calm, alert baby in the middle of a wake window, keep the room quiet, stop immediately if your baby fusses or turns away, and keep the session short. Three clean minutes with a happy baby are more useful than ten messy minutes with an overtired one. And before you start, decide what would make you end the session early. That way you're following your baby's state, not your own curiosity.

visual_notes: Chapter title export built on the peekaboo base plate.
narration: Experiment one is peekaboo, but done as a real protocol.

visual_notes: Use the base plate without labels so the scene feels warm and lived-in.
narration: In the 1970s, Jerome Bruner argued that peekaboo works because it has a rigid structure: disappearance, suspense, and reveal. That repetitive structure gives babies something they can start to predict. What looks casual from the outside is actually a tiny repeated script, and babies seem to learn the shape of that script very quickly.

visual_notes: Protocol overlay on the three-panel variation strip or a crop of it.
narration: At home, keep the script identical for three or four trials: eye contact, the same ready cue, cover your face from the center, pause for two seconds, then reveal with the same warm expression. The point of keeping the words and timing steady is that predictability gives the baby something to lock onto.

visual_notes: Show the labeled three-panel strip: standard, delayed, wrong-side.
narration: Once that baseline is stable, change one thing. Delay the reveal. Reveal from the wrong side. Or make the center briefly empty before you appear.

visual_notes: Stay on the labeled variation strip and crop into the baby's attention cues.
narration: What you're watching for is anticipation: a smile before the reveal, body tension during the pause, or eyes going to the place they expected you to be.

visual_notes: Return to the clean base plate for a softer landing.
narration: The point is not that your baby understands the game in an adult sense. It's that even this simple social routine is already giving them a structure they can learn and predict.

visual_notes: Chapter title export built on the calm still-face setup plate.
narration: Experiment two is the still-face paradigm, and this one is optional because it's the most sensitive.

visual_notes: Neutral setup image only. No distress shown.
narration: Tronick's classic finding was that when a normally responsive parent suddenly goes neutral and unresponsive, babies protest quickly.

visual_notes: Phase overlay built on the three-panel still-face strip.
narration: The home version is simple: one minute of normal warm play, up to thirty seconds of still face, then immediate reunion. Even a brief neutral face can feel surprisingly long when you're the parent, which is why it helps to think of this as a tightly bounded observation, not an endurance exercise.

visual_notes: Stay on the same overlay so the stop-early banner lands clearly.
narration: If your baby cries, turns red, or if you feel uncomfortable, stop right away. The reunion is not optional.

visual_notes: Return to the calm setup plate. Avoid anything that reads as harsh or dramatic.
narration: What this shows is not anything about attachment quality or your relationship. It shows that by around four to five months, babies already expect social partners to respond contingently. When that pattern breaks, they notice.

visual_notes: Chapter title export built on the wide play-gym room plate.
narration: Experiment three may be the most visually rewarding.

visual_notes: Wide supervised room shot only. Keep the parent visible in frame.
narration: In the classic mobile studies, babies learned that kicking made something interesting happen overhead. At home, the safest version uses a floor play gym, a soft ribbon, and full hands-on supervision. This one is especially satisfying because the change, if it happens, is often visible right in front of you.

visual_notes: Safe-setup overlay on the wide room plate. Keep callouts simple.
narration: First, watch two minutes of normal kicking with no connection. Then add a loose connection so kicking moves the toy. Then remove it again.

visual_notes: Use the labeled three-panel strip for baseline, connected, and disconnect phases.
narration: The observation lives in the contrast between those phases. Does kicking become more deliberate when the toy responds? After you disconnect it, do you see a burst of kicks as if your baby is trying to make the effect happen again? You're not looking for a perfect laboratory effect. You're looking for a noticeable shift from random movement to movement that seems organized around the toy.

visual_notes: Return to the wide room plate for the interpretive caution.
narration: That's contingency detection: noticing that when I do this, that happens. It's remarkable on its own. You don't need to inflate it into a claim about intelligence.

visual_notes: Chapter title export built on the imitation demo plate.
narration: Experiment four is imitation, and here the careful version matters.

visual_notes: Demonstration plate with the object clearly visible between caregiver and baby.
narration: The flashy claim that newborns imitate faces has not held up especially well. The stronger evidence comes a little later, with object actions.

visual_notes: Use the comparison overlay built on the delayed-sequence plate.
narration: Start with the easiest version: perform an unusual action with a rattle or cup three times, then hand it over and wait silently. A partial copy counts.

visual_notes: Stay on the same comparison overlay while covering delay and object-return variants.
narration: For older babies, you can add a short delay: demonstrate, put the object away, do something else for ten minutes, then bring it back with no reminder. That delay matters because it separates simple copying in the moment from remembering an action later. The hardest and most famous version uses a puppet or mitten with a bell inside. After a delay, does the baby do something that makes it jingle?

visual_notes: Return to the clean delayed-sequence plate and let the retention idea breathe.
narration: What matters here is not perfect copying. It's whether your baby retained some representation of the action they saw.

visual_notes: Chapter title export built on the notebook plate.
narration: The difference between a home experiment and random play is how you write it down.

visual_notes: Warm notebook close-up. Add only the observation-versus-interpretation contrast as overlay if needed.
narration: Record what you saw, not what you think it means. Baby kicked more during the connected phase is an observation. Baby understood cause and effect is an interpretation. The most honest notes are concrete: smiled before reveal, looked at center first, kicked more after disconnect, reached for the puppet after the delay.

visual_notes: Use the Keynote note template built on the notebook plate.
narration: Timing, temperament, and sheer luck are huge confounders. The same baby can look brilliant in the morning and bored in the afternoon. A single trial is interesting. A pattern across different days is much more informative. If you repeat the same setup on different days and start seeing the same pattern, that's when the observation becomes more persuasive.

visual_notes: Start on the end card, then briefly cut through earlier Nano Banana plates before landing back here.
on_screen_text: Prediction • social expectation • cause and effect • memory
narration: What I love about these experiments is not that they prove something grand. It's that they make early cognition visible.

visual_notes: Use a short montage of hero, peekaboo, still-face, kick, and imitation plates before settling on the article CTA end card.
on_screen_text: Read the full guide at rl-field-notes.vercel.app
narration: Peekaboo lets you watch prediction. Still-face reveals social expectation. Kick-to-move shows cause and effect. Imitation gives you a glimpse of memory. If you want the full article, diagrams, and study citations, I'll link it below. And if you end up trying one of these at home, the rule is simple: keep it safe, keep it short, and stay curious.
